result:
I want to write about the moment the sky forgets its color.  
Not a metaphor for apocalypse, not the hazy salmon-grey that hangs over Delhi in November when the stubble burns and the lungs of ten million children tighten like tiny fists. I mean the literal instant when the compound eye of the sky—normally so fastidious—loses the word “blue,” the way a sleepwalker misplaces the staircase and keeps walking. One second the stratosphere remembers cerulean, the next it doesn’t. The color drains upward, like water sucked out of a sink the size of the world, and what is left is a perfectly transparent pane you can press your face against and see straight into the machinery of space. No black, no indigo, no bruise violet—just the working gears: satellites ticking, the moon’s white fuse, the sun a welding torch with the guard lifted off. After the first panic (pilots screaming into radios, Instagram melting into a single neon question mark), the collective human conclusion is that the sky has not died; it has only become honest. It has stepped out from behind the pigmentation it wore for every artist who ever needed a backdrop. We are, for the first time, backstage.

I was on the 43rd floor of a glass tower in Jakarta when it happened, eating a lukewarm tuna sandwich that cost more than the daily wage of the woman who swabbed the corridor. The windows turned into lenses. My reflection vanished, along with the reflection of the city. Across the void, I could see other buildings standing in their undergarments—steel ligaments, elevator cables like Achilles tendons, conference rooms where men in pastel shirts were looking up as if God had yanked the curtain off the shower. Someone started praying in Arabic, someone else in Korean. A third person, a white guy in a suit that looked expensive even from behind, began taking selfies, because that is what you do when the sky forgets its color: you establish evidence that you were there, that you mattered, that the algorithm should remember you favorably. I did nothing. I licked a fleck of sweet onion off my thumb and felt the vertigo of a creature whose bowl has been removed. Fish-on-dock sensation. No sky, no lid, no mercy. Yet the air still tasted of clove cigarettes and monsoon drains; the earth still spun obediently. Only the ceiling had been confiscated.

By the time I reached street level, the city had organized itself into two religions. The Verticals refused to look up; they walked stare-locked at the grid of sidewalks, white cords in their ears pumping lo-fi beats to drown out the cosmic leakage. The Horizontals stood on roofs, hoods of cars, folding chairs dragged out from warungs, necks craned so far back that the cervical disks screamed. Their pupils dilated like black market tunnels. Both groups were reverent, but only the Horizontals were happy. They whispered “akhirnya” — finally — as though they had waited their whole lives for the roof to come off, for the embarrassing wallpaper called sky to be stripped. I joined them without choosing. When you have no theology left, you follow the people whose eyes are wet with gratitude.

Night arrived without pigment, the way a clerk arrives at the office when the lights are still off. You could see the sun folding itself into a suitcase, click-click, and boarding the express to the other side of the planet. But there was no gradient, no Rothko fade. Just a toggle: day ON / day OFF. The stars did not “come out”; they had never left. They stood there all afternoon like rude guests who arrive before the host and drink straight from the bottles. Now they blinked once, apologetic, and resumed burning. Someone started playing a gamelan ringtone, and the metallic plinks felt appropriate, as if Java itself were trying to soundtrack its own unraveling.

I walked north, toward the old colonial port, because ships know how to live without ceilings; they bring their own horizon. The road passed through Glodok, Chinatown, where red lanterns swung uselessly—what is red when there is no red sky to contradict it? I realized that every color down here had been borrowed from up there, the way children borrow prestige from their parents’ last names. Take away the sky’s cerulean and the shopfront turquoise suddenly looks fraudulent, like a drag queen caught in daylight. Even blood looked different pooling on the asphalt after a scooter clipped a woman’s knee. Dark silver. Mercury with secrets.

At the mouth of the harbor, the container cranes had stopped mid-breath. They loomed like giraffes interrupted while drinking, necks angled, hooks dangling. Beyond them, the sea. It had not forgot its color—salt water is too proud to obey atmospheric amnesia—but it reflected the new see-through dome above it, so that when you stared at the waves you saw two abysses shaking hands: one liquid, one vacuum. I felt the way a bookmark must feel when the book vanishes: still aligned with a narrative that has disappeared.

I sat on a bollard. My phone had lost signal, but it pretended otherwise, the bar graph stuck on two courageous white sticks. I wrote an email to my mother anyway. Subject: Don’t panic, but the sky. Body: …is gone. I spared her the metaphors. When I pressed send, the phone produced the usual whoosh sound, a sonic fig leaf. I sensed the message would land in some dead-letter office in the troposphere, sorted by an angel who no longer recognized the address “Earth.”

A boy approached, barefoot, carrying a birdcage made of soda cans. Inside, instead of a myna or a dove, was a small mirror. He held it up to me. “Look,” he said in Bahasa, “this is what the sky saw when it looked down.” I peered in. All I saw was my own face, but younger, the way I appeared in passport photos taken right before a long journey. The boy grinned, exposing adult incisors too large for his jaw, and ran off. The cage swung, and the mirror caught fragments of starlight, scattering them like seed. I understood then that the sky’s amnesia was contagious; it was sliding down into us, erasing the color we kept in private storage. My own memories were desaturating. The green of my first bicycle—gone. The yellow of the dress my lover wore the night she said maybe we should take a break—fading. I tried to summon that yellow and could only produce a polygon with the label “yellow” attached, like a placeholder in a corrupted file.

Hours compressed. Timekeeping had always relied on sky cues: sunrise, sunset, the slow swivel of shadows. Without hues, duration became elastic, a rubber band handled by a bored child. I felt I had been sitting on the bollard for three minutes and also for the entire Cenozoic era. My knees ached like limestone. A ship’s horn sounded, a low B-flat that traveled through the deck plates of the earth and up my spine. The sound had no echo; echoes need walls, and we were fresh out.

Eventually I stood and walked back into the city. Jakarta had become a diorama of itself. Food carts glowed under LED lamps that now produced colors they had no names for—ultra-sky, minus-red, void-lavender. People ate anyway. Satay sauce still stuck to mustaches. Neon still buzzed like horny cicadas. Survival is a cafeteria: you grab what’s open, you don’t complain about the menu. I passed a mosque where the imam had rolled out the loudspeaker and aimed it at the transparency. He recited the iqama, the call to prayer, but the words went up and up, past where the sky used to be, and kept going. Maybe they would hitch a ride on a meteor. Maybe they would become the new constellations, syllables hardening into light. The congregation prayed on the sidewalk, foreheads touching plastic woven mats that smelled of warehouse polyethylene. Their asses pointed at the emptiness, an inadvertent mooning of the divine. I loved them for it.

I needed to find my lover. Not for reunion, not for closure—just to see whether her face had kept its color. I took an ojek, a motorcycle taxi, driven by a woman with a pink helmet that now looked the shade of wet newspaper. She drove as if the road were a zipper she was eager to close. We weaved through traffic lights that cycled through colors we could no longer read. Green meant go, but what exactly was green? A wavelength, a social contract, a memory of leaves. I held on to the driver’s jacket, a counterfeit varsity thing that said “Harvard” in peeling felt. The wind tasted metallic, like licking a battery.

Her apartment was in Kuningan, one of those blocks where the pool is on the 7th floor and the gym faces southeast so treadmills can watch sunrises that no longer exist. The elevator mirror showed me my pupils: huge, starving moons. I knocked. She opened wearing overalls spattered with acrylic. Behind her, canvases leaned like drunk diplomats. She had been painting the new sky, or trying. Tubes of paint lay decapitated. The palette was a crime scene. On the easel: a rectangle of nothing, framed. She had captured it perfectly—the transparency, the refusal to be color. I asked if she still knew the word “yellow.” She said yellow had become like the name of a childhood friend who moved away: you remember the games, but the face is gone. She poured me water. It tasted like the future, which is to say tasteless. We sat on the balcony. Below, the pool reflected the stars, a reverse planetarium. Someone floated on an inflatable unicorn, eyes open, drifting toward the drainage grate. We didn’t touch. We didn’t need to. The absence of sky had already put its arm around both of us.

She told me she had started receiving messages on her phone. Not texts, not voice, just colors flickering on the screen, one per day. Yesterday a square of burnt umber. Today, something close to teal. She swore they arrived at 11:11 local time. “Maybe the sky is texting us its last will and testament,” she said. “Or maybe it’s trying to remember.” I stared at her screen. Black. While we watched, a pixel coughed, then a weak blush of coral spread, lasted three seconds, collapsed. We both exhaled, as if we had been holding breath for years.

I left at nil o’clock. The streets were liquid with people who had given up sleeping because darkness no longer cycled with light. Night was now a permanent administrative position. Hawkers sold coffee thick as bitumen. A girl offered me a glow stick, but when I cracked it, the liquid inside remained clear. She shrugged: “The light is there, we just can’t see it.” I paid her anyway, clipped the empty stick to my shirt like a medal.

By the time I reached the National Monument, Monas, the obelisk rose into the void like a hypodermic trying to vaccinate the universe. A crowd had gathered. They stood in concentric circles, thousands of necks craned. No one spoke. At the base, a man in a vendor’s vest distributed paper airplanes made from old receipts. People launched them. The planes flew upward, became tiny white scars, then nothing. It was the most beautiful funeral I had ever attended for something that wasn’t dead.

I kept walking north until the asphalt ended and the fishing boats began. The sand was black volcanic powder; it, too, had lost its contrast, looked now like spilled ink that refused to dry. I took off my shoes. The grains were warm from geothermal memory. I waded into the water. Tiny phosphorescent plankton, confused by the starlight from above and below, blinked like faulty Christmas lights. I kept going until the shorts stuck to my thighs. The horizon had become a Möbius strip; you could no longer tell where sea ended and space began. I dove. Eyes open. Salt bit. There, suspended between two immensities, I finally understood what the sky’s amnesia was teaching us: color is not property, it’s diplomacy. Blue was never the sky’s “color”; it was the treaty it signed with human eyes. Break the treaty and you discover that the world was never painted—only patinaed with consensus.

I surfaced, gasping. To the east, a thin band of something was leaking back into the dome. Not blue—never blue again—but a raw, newborn hue that existed nowhere in my retina’s back catalog. It looked like the sound of a bell before it becomes sound. The Horizontals on the beach saw it too. A collective inhale. Phones rose like periscopes. But no filter could capture it. The color existed only in the act of being seen, vanished the instant the brain tried to name it. Maybe that is grace: un-transferable, un-ownable, like the kiss of someone who will never love you back.

I walked out of the water, clothes heavy as guilt. The first vendors of dawn-un-dawn hawked instant noodles with flavor packets labeled “Sky.” I bought one, slurped it sitting on a beached catamaran. The broth tasted of iron, ozone, and the small sadness of realizing that every story you ever told yourself about permanence was just a lullaby hummed by a species that happened to evolve under a blue ceiling. I forgave myself. Slurped louder. Around me, Jakarta began to rebuild its noise: engines, muezzin, sparrows that sang even when they couldn’t see the sun. Life is stubborn; it keeps its appointments.

I decided to keep walking until the road ran out of names. I would become a Horizontal forever, neck hinged upward, pupils wide as subway tunnels. I would collect the new colors the way monks collect begging bowls, one day at a time, 11:11, pixel by pixel. Maybe somewhere along the unnamed highway I would meet the boy with the mirror cage, and we would trade: my future face for his bird of light. Maybe my mother would receive my letter and paste it into her refrigerator door, proof that her son once witnessed the sky undress and did not look away. Maybe my lover would keep painting rectangles of nothing until one of them blinked, admitted her, and she would step through, leaving behind a yellow dress fluttering on the easel like a surrender flag.

Or maybe none of that. Maybe I would simply walk until the shoes dissolved, until the ankles became roots, until the kelongs—those offshore fishing platforms—took me in as a post, a barnacled witness to tides that no longer needed the moon’s permission. Children would climb me, paint me with leftover hues that still had no names. I would stand there, a totem to the day the sky forgot its color and we, imperfect diplomats, learned to negotiate with the naked dark.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
